cards.title: The Fundamental Difference: Guided vs Standalone
cards.teaser: In a live presentation you are the chart's narrator. In a sent document the chart has to narrate itself — and most charts are designed for only one of those modes.

runs.text: The deepest divide in data visualization is not bar chart vs line chart, or color choice, or axis labels. It is: does a human voice accompany this chart, or does it stand alone?

A live chart is guided. The presenter points at a spike and says "this is when we deployed the new algorithm." The chart doesn't need to explain the spike — the speaker explains the spike, and the chart is the visual anchor that focuses attention while they do.

A sent chart is standalone. The recipient opens it at 9am, 3pm, or 11pm, in a tab surrounded by seventeen other things, with no voice in the room. Every label, annotation, and title must do the work the presenter would have done.
Most communication failures happen because someone designed a chart for one mode and delivered it in the other:

items.text: A slide chart emailed to a team: dense with unlabeled callouts the presenter meant to explain verbally, confusing without that explanation.
A document-quality chart dropped into a slide: over-annotated, too much text, fights for attention instead of directing it.

runs.text: The first design decision for any chart is not visual — it is: where does this land, and who (or what) will guide the audience through it?
The same underlying data often needs two charts: one for the room and one for the inbox. Building both from the same source is fine. Trying to use one for both is where the confusion starts.
Like a code comment vs a commit message — same information, but one is read in-context by someone already looking at the code, the other has to make sense in isolation weeks later.

cards.title: Chart Density for Slides vs Docs
cards.teaser: Slides want fewer elements. Documents want more complete annotation. The right density for one format is actively wrong for the other.

runs.text: Density is the number of elements asking for the audience's attention at the same moment: data series, labels, annotations, legend entries, footnotes, gridlines.

In a live slide, density is the enemy. The audience is processing spoken words, slide visuals, and their own thoughts simultaneously. A chart with five labeled data series, a legend, three callout annotations, and a footnote will lose most of the room — they'll either read the chart or listen to the speaker, not both. Slide charts should present one idea per chart with the minimum elements needed to make that idea visible.

In a sent document, thin annotation is the enemy. A clean, minimal chart dropped into an email or report forces the reader to infer what matters, what changed, and what they should do with this information. That inference work is friction. Friction causes readers to give up or misinterpret.
Practical density calibration:

items.text: Slides: one primary data series in focus, secondary series visually receded, no more than one annotation, title is a declarative sentence that names the conclusion.
Documents: all relevant series labeled at the line endpoint or in a legend, annotations explain both what happened and why it matters, title is descriptive but the caption carries the interpretation.

runs.text: The mistake is not adding too much or too little — it is applying the wrong density standard for the delivery format.
A useful test: cover the speaker's portion and ask whether the chart still communicates the point. If yes, it may be over-annotated for a slide. If no, it may be under-annotated for a document.
Like function signatures with and without inline documentation — the right level of annotation depends on whether the reader has surrounding context or is arriving cold.

cards.title: Annotation Strategy in Live Presentations
cards.teaser: In a live presentation, callouts and titles are verbal cues the speaker refers to — not labels that must explain themselves.

runs.text: Annotations in a live slide serve a different purpose than annotations in a document. They are not there to communicate information independently. They are there to give the speaker a shared reference point with the audience.

When a presenter says "look at the callout on the right side of the chart" or "notice the shaded region in Q3," the annotation's job is to make it easy for the audience to find and focus on what the speaker is pointing to. The annotation directs attention; the voice delivers meaning.
This changes how you design live annotations:

items.text: Short labels, not explanations. "Deployment" is enough. "Deployment of v2.1 that caused a 40% latency increase" belongs in the speaker's words, not a callout box.
Spatial clarity over completeness. The annotation should be unambiguous about what it is marking. It does not need to be a complete sentence.

Chart title as a signal, not a summary. "Q3 Latency" tells the audience what they're looking at. "Q3 Latency spiked after v2.1 deploy" is acceptable if the speaker elaborates, but it front-loads the conclusion before the audience has seen the chart — which can feel like the presenter telling them what to think before they've had a moment to see.

runs.text: The risk with live annotations: they become meaningless when the deck is shared async. A callout that says "this" only communicates when someone is pointing at a thing in the room.
Annotations that reference the presenter's words — "as I mentioned," "the red arrow shows what we discussed" — are invisible when the deck is emailed without the presenter.
Like comments in a live code review: "see here — this is the bug" makes sense when you're both in the diff, but is useless in a code review opened a week later without context.

cards.title: Annotation Strategy in Sent Documents
cards.teaser: In a sent document, callouts and titles replace the speaker entirely — they must deliver both the observation and the interpretation.

runs.text: A sent document has no room for ambiguity. Every annotation must do the work that a presenter would do in the room: direct attention, name what is happening, and tell the reader what it means.
This is a fundamentally higher annotation burden than live presentations, and most charts fail at it because their creators default to the live presentation style even when the medium is async.
What document annotations must include:

items.text: A descriptive title that states the main finding, not just the topic. "Monthly active users declined 18% in March" beats "Monthly Active Users, Jan–Apr." The reader does not have time to derive the finding themselves.
Callouts that are complete sentences. Not "spike" — "Spike on March 14 coincides with the payment processor outage." The annotation is a standalone observation.
A caption that frames interpretation. Below the chart, one to two sentences: what happened, and what the reader should think or do about it.
Labeled data series. The reader cannot ask "which line is which?" — the chart must answer that without a legend they have to cross-reference.

runs.text: The calibration error in the other direction is over-annotation: cramming so much text into callouts that the visual becomes a wall of words. That breaks the chart for a different reason — the reader cannot extract a signal from the noise. The discipline is completeness without redundancy.
If the reader has to stare at a sent chart for more than thirty seconds to understand the main point, the annotation strategy has failed.
Like a README vs inline code documentation — the README has to work without running the code, so it has to say more up front.

cards.title: The Leave-Behind Document
cards.teaser: A leave-behind is a version of the deck designed to work without a presenter — it is not the same file.

runs.text: The leave-behind is the document distributed to people who were not in the room, or who were but want to review the material independently. Most teams send the presentation deck and call it the leave-behind. These are different things.

A presentation deck is optimized for delivery: sparse slides, speaker-guided annotation, titles that signal rather than explain. That file, opened in isolation, is disorienting. The slides that made perfect sense while a presenter walked through them look incomplete or cryptic when read alone.
A leave-behind document is designed for solo consumption. It can use the same charts and the same structure, but:

items.text: Every chart must meet document annotation standards: declarative titles, labeled series, complete callouts, interpretive captions.
Speaker notes become visible text — either inline, as a running commentary below each chart, or reformatted as a narrative document.
Progressive reveals and animations are collapsed — the reader sees the full chart, not a partially built version.
The logical flow is made explicit with transition text between sections, because no presenter is there to say "so that brings us to the next topic."

runs.text: Building leave-behinds properly takes time. The practical options:

items.text: Write a separate document version of the deck (most work, best result).
Export the deck with notes pages enabled (medium effort, imperfect result).
Add a cover email or summary section that provides the framing the presenter would have given (minimal effort, works for simple decks).

runs.text: The test: hand the document to someone who was not in the meeting. If they can reconstruct the main argument and conclusions without asking you questions, the leave-behind is working.
Like a conference talk vs the accompanying blog post — the same material, but one needs a human in the room and the other has to work on a screen at midnight.

cards.title: Speaking Notes and Chart Design
cards.teaser: When you plan to narrate a chart, you can design it more sparsely — but you are creating a fragile dependency between the chart and the speaker.

runs.text: Speaking notes change the design budget for a chart. If you plan to explain the spike verbally, you do not need to annotate the spike in the chart. If you plan to walk through each data series in turn, you do not need a legend with detailed labels. The speaker's words carry information that the chart does not have to carry.
This creates a real design advantage in live settings: sparse, clear charts that breathe. The visual is a canvas the speaker paints meaning onto.
The risk is that this creates a dependency. The chart only works when the intended speaker delivers the intended words. That dependency breaks in predictable ways:

items.text: The presenter is sick and a colleague presents the deck without preparation.
The deck is forwarded to a stakeholder who was not in the meeting.
The speaker skips a section or changes the narrative midway through.
A slide is screenshotted and shared in Slack, divorced from the rest of the deck.

runs.text: Designing with speaking notes in mind is not wrong. It is the correct approach for a well-prepared live presentation with controlled distribution. The error is treating speaking notes as a substitute for chart annotation without acknowledging the fragility that creates.
If the deck has any chance of leaving the controlled live context — forwarded, recorded, archived — the annotation burden shifts back toward self-sufficiency.
Speaking notes are not stored in the chart file. They live in the presenter's head. The chart without the presenter is not the same artifact.
Like runtime configuration that only exists in memory — it works while the process is running, but it is gone the moment the process stops.

cards.title: The "Deck as Document" Failure
cards.teaser: Sending a presentation deck as a document is one of the most common communication failures in organizations — it looks like sharing information but often obscures it.

runs.text: Organizations default to the deck because it is fast to produce, tool support is universal (PowerPoint, Keynote, Google Slides), and it is the format decisions get made in. The deck becomes the canonical artifact of a project or analysis, and so it gets shared as the canonical reference document.
The failure mode is structural, not cosmetic:

items.text: Slide titles are designed for attention, not navigation. A deck titled "Q3 Retrospective" with slides titled "The Challenge," "What We Did," and "What's Next" is coherent in a presentation where context builds. As a document someone reads six months later, these titles mean nothing.
Charts without annotations make no sense without the speaker. The data is present, but the interpretation is gone.
Bullet-point slides without full sentences strip the reasoning. "Latency — fixed" is a slide note, not a finding.
The narrative requires sequential consumption, but documents are typically skimmed non-linearly. Someone jumping to slide 14 without slides 1–13 will be lost.

runs.text: This failure persists because the people who sent the deck were in the meeting and understood it. They received the same artifact they sent and it made sense to them. The failure is invisible to the sender and fully visible to the recipient.

When a deck is the canonical reference for a decision, future team members will try to reconstruct context from slides that were never designed to hold that context. This is a common source of institutional knowledge loss.
Like reading the changelog commits without the linked issue threads — the summary is there, but the reasoning is missing.

cards.title: Progressive Reveal in Slides
cards.teaser: Building a chart element by element is powerful live — it controls attention and pacing. Statically, it produces a confusing half-built chart.

runs.text: Progressive reveal is the technique of animating a chart into view one piece at a time: first the axes, then one data series, then a callout, then a second series. In a live presentation it is a legitimate tool for controlling audience attention. By showing only the line you want the audience to look at, you prevent their eyes from jumping ahead to the comparison you have not narrated yet.
Done well, progressive reveal makes a complex chart legible in stages. Done badly, it just delays the inevitable moment when the full chart is confusing.

The problem: a chart built with progressive reveal exported to PDF, shared as a screenshot, or included in a leave-behind document shows only the final state — or worse, one of the intermediate states if the export captured the wrong animation frame. Neither is designed to be read without the animation sequence.
When designing with progressive reveal:

items.text: The final-state version of the chart (all elements visible) must be legible on its own. If the full chart is confusing without narration, progressive reveal is covering up a design problem.
The intermediate states are valid only in the live context. They cannot be exported and used as standalone charts in a document.
For the leave-behind, collapse the reveal and treat the chart as a single static artifact.

runs.text: Progressive reveal borrowed from presentation software to code demos and data walkthroughs has the same constraint: it requires a live driver. Screenshots of terminals mid-demo are not documentation.
If the final-state chart still requires a presenter to be legible, the chart has a design problem that progressive reveal cannot solve.
Like a live coding demo that builds a function line by line — powerful to watch in real time, meaningless as a screenshot of line 7 of 20.

cards.title: Charts in Written Documents
cards.teaser: A chart in a written document carries a caption and context burden that slides do not — and most charts borrowed from slide decks do not meet that standard.

runs.text: A chart embedded in a written document — report, RFC, design doc, wiki page — exists in a different reading environment than a slide. The reader is scanning prose, encountering the chart as an interruption of the narrative, and deciding how much time to spend on it before returning to the text.
The requirements this creates:

items.text: The chart must have a figure number or label so the surrounding text can reference it precisely. "See Figure 3" is more useful than "as shown above" when the document will be read in different renderings or page layouts.

The caption must stand alone. A reader who reads only the caption should walk away with the main finding. The caption is not a title — it is a one to two sentence summary: what the chart shows and what it means.

The chart title and axes must be fully labeled without assuming prior context from surrounding text. A reader who screenshots the chart and shares it elsewhere should not need the document context to understand the basic setup.

Data source and date range belong in the caption or footnote, not as an implicit assumption. "Data as of April 2025, sourced from production analytics database" prevents misapplication of the chart to future decisions where the data is stale.

runs.text: The common failure: a chart designed for a slide (sparse, declarative title, no caption) dropped inline into a report. The prose carries the interpretation, the chart is a visual prop, and the chart alone communicates almost nothing.

Read the caption without looking at the chart. Then look at the chart without reading the caption. Both should communicate the main point — they reinforce each other rather than each depending on the other.
Like a figure in a research paper — the caption has to be self-contained because reviewers will read it out of order, copy it, and cite it independently from the surrounding text.

cards.title: The Napkin Math Chart
cards.teaser: The roughest useful visualization in the world is the sketch drawn in a meeting or whiteboard — and it plays by completely different rules than any polished chart.

runs.text: Not every chart is a finished artifact. Some charts exist only to communicate an order-of-magnitude relationship in a working session: a quick sketch of a funnel, a back-of-napkin growth curve, a whiteboard diagram showing how two metrics relate. These are napkin math charts.
Napkin math charts have different rules:

items.text: Precision is irrelevant. The point is relative magnitude, not accurate representation. A bar that is roughly twice as tall as another bar is doing its job if the actual ratio is 2.3:1.
Labels are minimal and verbal. "Big," "small," "fast," "slow" are valid labels when the sketch is a communication aid in a live discussion, not a record.
Impermanence is acceptable. A whiteboard photo is not a permanent artifact. It should be re-created properly if it needs to live beyond the meeting.
Axes can be implied. In a sketch, the audience infers what is on the axes from the conversation. This is only valid in the room.

runs.text: The failure mode is treating a napkin math chart as a real chart: photographing it, adding it to a deck, sharing it in a document, and letting it substitute for a properly constructed visualization. The rough sketch that made an argument in the room becomes misleading evidence in async context because the imprecision and implied labels are invisible to someone who was not in the meeting.
Whiteboard photos in shared documents often look like evidence. They are rough working sketches. Label them as such or redraw them before sharing.
Like a pseudocode sketch in a design meeting — useful for communicating the idea in the room, not a substitute for the actual implementation spec.

cards.title: Choosing the Format Before Creating Anything
cards.teaser: The format decision — deck, doc, or chart — comes before the first slide or visualization. Making it last causes rework and produces artifacts designed for the wrong medium.

runs.text: Most analysts and engineers default to the tool they know: open a Jupyter notebook, build charts, then figure out where they land. Or open a Google Slides template because the output is "a presentation." The format decision gets made by habit, not intent.
The right sequence is the opposite. Before creating anything:

items.text: Who is the audience, and will they be in a room together or receiving this async?
What decision or action does this output need to drive?
What is the expected shelf life — consumed once in a meeting, or referenced for months?
What is the audience's baseline context — do they need orientation, or can you start at the finding?

runs.text: These questions determine the format:

items.text: Live synchronous audience, moderate context, single-use: slide deck with live presenter.
Async audience, mixed context, referenced over time: written document with embedded charts.
Technical audience in a working session, shared context, exploratory: Jupyter notebook or whiteboard.
Executive audience, async, decision to drive: one-page document with one primary chart, declarative title, specific recommendation.

runs.text: Making this decision explicitly also makes the artifact better. A document designed to be a document from the start is more navigable than a deck forced into document form afterward. A slide deck designed to be presented is sparser and clearer than a deck that was built to double as a report.

"We can just use the deck" is the most common reason the leave-behind does not work. The deck was not designed to stand alone, and retrofitting it costs more than designing for the right format from the beginning.
Like choosing between a CLI tool, a REST API, and a web UI before writing a line of code — the interface contract determines the whole design, and changing it later is expensive.

cards.title: Anti-Pattern: The Deck Sent as a Document
cards.teaser: Sending a presentation deck to people who were not in the room, without a presenter, is an anti-pattern that looks like communication but frequently isn't.

runs.text: This is the most common chart communication failure in engineering and data organizations, and it persists because it is invisible to the people who cause it.
The pattern:

items.text: A team builds a deck to present a quarterly review, architecture decision, or data analysis.
The deck is presented live, and the meeting goes well — questions are answered, context is established in the room.
The deck is emailed to stakeholders who were not in the meeting, or shared in a Slack channel "for those who couldn't attend."

Recipients open a slide deck designed for live narration, encounter sparse charts with unexplained callouts, bullet points without complete sentences, and a narrative that requires the live context to make sense.
Recipients form their own interpretations, ask clarifying questions, or — most commonly — do not engage and move on.

runs.text: Why it persists:

items.text: The senders were in the meeting and understood the deck perfectly. The artifact makes sense to them.
Sending a deck is one step. Building a proper leave-behind is many steps.
"I'll just add some speaker notes" is the middle path that satisfies neither use case — speaker notes are not read by people who receive the deck asynchronously.

runs.text: The fix is not cosmetic. It requires accepting that a presentation deck and a shared document are different artifacts that need different designs. For high-stakes communications — funding decisions, major architectural choices, post-mortems shared broadly — build both.

For lower-stakes communications, at minimum write a cover message that delivers the key findings, context, and recommendation as prose. The deck then serves as supporting detail for those who want to verify the work, not as the primary communication vehicle.
A shared deck without a presenter is not communication. It is the raw material of communication — the data without the interpretation. Treat it as such.
Like pushing a Git branch without a pull request description and calling it a code review — the code is there, but the reasoning, context, and recommendation that make it reviewable are missing.
